text: The surgeon begins the hysterectomy by placing a primary umbilical 10 mm optical trocar and four secondary working ports. The surgeon stands on the left side of the patient, working ipsilaterally, while the assistant uses two right-sided ipsilateral ports. The procedure starts with the dissection of pelvic spaces, opening the peritoneum below the right infundibulopelvic ligament and proceeding towards the rectovaginal space. The harmonic scalpel is used for precise dissection close to the rectal fat.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the two layers of the Denonvilliers fascia, developing the rectovaginal space. The dissection continues on the left side, focusing on the left pararectal space. The harmonic scalpel is used to make precise incisions in the peritoneum, revealing the ureter and tracing the division of the internal iliac vascular system.

The surgeon applies traction forces parallel to the orientation of the vessels, revealing the uterine artery crossing the pararectal fossa, marked by a red arrow. Millimeter by millimeter dissection is performed, further opening the peritoneum and enlarging the spaces. The uterine artery is ligated at its origin using the three clip method with a harmonic scalpel. The deep uterine vein is visible at a deeper level, marked by a blue dot, situated below the uterine artery and slightly dorsomedial to the inferior hypogastric plexus containing the pelvic splanchnic nerves.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the pelvic spaces by further opening the peritoneum, focusing on developing the pararectal space on the right side. The incision is made slightly superior to the right infundibulopelvic ligament. The left hand uses a Maryland grasper to tent the peritoneum, facilitating precise dissection and allowing gas to enter and expand the natural planes.

The surgeon further develops the right pararectal space, then opens the round ligament near the pelvic side wall to access the retroperitoneum. The round ligament is cut parallel to the right infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament, which is then skeletonized. A similar procedure is performed on the left side, opening the round ligament laterally and developing the IP ligament. The surgeon proceeds to develop the vesico-vaginal space anteriorly.

The surgeon extends the peritoneum cut anteriorly, lifting the fold to develop the vesico-vaginal space and push the bladder caudally. The bladder pillar is taken at the level of the cervix. The infundibulopelvic ligament is ligated using a vessel sealing device, with the pelvic triangle opened on the right side. The right infundibulopelvic ligament is ligated, and the left infundibulopelvic ligament is divided. The uterine artery is divided at the level of the internal os.

The surgeon performs an extra-fascial hysterectomy without manipulating the uterus, with assistant graspers holding it in place. The resection of the para cervix is midway between the cervix and the ureter, marked by an oblique red line. The vagina is opened, and the cervix is circumferentially excised, leaving a small vaginal margin of approximately five millimeters. The entire cervix is included in the specimen, which is the surgical goal.

The surgeon makes precise cuts into the vagina, avoiding the cervix, to perform the extra facial hysterectomy. The procedure progresses to the pelvic lymph node dissection, where the peritoneum fold is hitched anteriorly towards the abdominal wall to provide better exposure and stretch on the lateral side, freeing the assistant grasper from maintaining tension on the fold.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the pelvic lymph nodes at the lateral border of the external iliac vascular system. Using a vessel sealing device, short bursts of energy are applied to develop the lateral border of the external iliac artery. The meticulous dissection exposes the external iliac vein, with the assistant grasper guarding the vein on the lateral side while the surgeon delineates the boundaries to release the external iliac group of lymph nodes.

The surgeon performs a pelvic lymph node dissection, defining the lateral limit at the genitofemoral nerve on the psoas muscle, the medial limit at the ureter, the inferior limit at the obturator nerve, and the superior limit at the bifurcation of the common iliac artery. The external iliac vein is exposed, and the dissection progresses towards the ilio-obturator group of lymph nodes. The obturator nerve is identified deep between the internal and external iliac vascular systems, with care taken to stay above it. The entire nodal chain is removed en bloc.

The surgeon proceeds with the dissection on the left side, encountering an enlarged lymph node in the external iliac group. Care is taken to excise the node intact without disrupting the nodal tissue, distinguishing it from surrounding fat by its stickier texture.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the ilio obturator group of lymph nodes. The obturator nerve is identified at a deep level, marked by a yellow arrow. The deep circumflex iliac vein, marked by oblique blue lines, is visible, showing its communication between the external iliac vein (broad blue lines) and the obturator vein below. The assistant uses a grasper to provide traction on the nodal mass, allowing for gentle division of the lymphatics.

The surgeon completes the closure of the vaginal vault using a number one vicryl suture on a round body needle, employing an ipsilateral suturing technique. The nodal masses are placed into an endobag and removed vaginally. The final view of the pelvis is shown after the completion of the pelvic lymphadenectomy.
